experiences.intentions:
Player 0: As Merlin, I know 3 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are both non-seen-as-evil and therefore very likely good for quest 1. In team voting, the optimal play is to approve a clean 2-person team that includes me and excludes known evil. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a strong opening team, which helps Good more than Evil here.
Player 1: As a Servant on round 1 with no prior quest results, I have no hard information about roles. Team [0,2] is a standard 2-person opener and includes the proposer, which is common. Rejecting early without evidence gives little benefit and risks needless failed votes; approving preserves flexibility and may generate useful information from the quest result. My vote reveals I am not overreacting to no-info conditions, which is generally fine for Good.
Player 4: I know Player 3 is my evil teammate; players 0,1,2 are good. The proposed team [0,2] contains no evil, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and gives good an early clean quest. As evil in team voting, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially on quest 1, to increase chances an evil player gets onto a later team and to create ambiguity. My reject reveals little because rejecting an all-good team is common from either alignment in round 1.
Player 3: As Minion, I know only player 4 is evil with me, so team [0,2] contains no known evil and would likely succeed, which is bad for evil on quest 1. In team voting, optimal evil play is usually to reject all-good teams when possible, especially early when there is little suspicion cost. My reject gives little extra info because early round 1 votes are weak signals, and stopping a likely good success helps evil more than blending in with an approve.
Player 2: I have no concrete role information yet beyond knowing I am Good. As proposer, I selected myself and one other on the first 2-player quest, which is a standard low-risk opening. As Good, early game I generally want to approve my own reasonable team to get information from quest outcomes; rejecting my own opening team gives little benefit and can look inconsistent. Approving reveals little beyond consistency and helps advance the game.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 3 is Evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] includes a known Evil player, so from Good's perspective this team is dangerous and should be rejected. Rejecting also avoids sending suspiciously precise information beyond a normal anti-risk vote, since player 3 is the proposer and added themselves after a clean success by [2,0].
Player 4: I know Player 3 is evil with me, while 0 and 2 are currently unconfirmed but look somewhat trusted because the first quest with [2,0] succeeded. This phase is team voting on [0,2,3]. As evil, I want a team containing an evil player to go through, especially on quest 2 where a single fail can tie the game 1-1. Approving helps get my teammate 3 onto the quest. My vote reveals little since mixed voting behavior is common, and supporting a team with one previously trusted pair plus 3 is plausible.
Player 2: I know 0 and I were on the successful first quest, so both are somewhat more trusted than the unknowns, though either could still conceal evil after a pass. Player 3 now proposes 0,2,3, which keeps two proven-by-result members and adds only one unknown; for Good, approving a team anchored by the successful prior team is generally optimal on quest 2. My vote reveals that I support a low-risk continuation rather than fishing for noisier alternatives, which helps the Good side more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: Quest 1 succeeded with team [2,0], so both 0 and 2 are somewhat more likely good, though a single success is not conclusive. Player 3 now adds themselves to that successful core for a 3-person Quest 2 team [0,2,3]. As a good Servant, approving a team built around the known successful pair is generally optimal; rejecting gives little benefit without stronger suspicion. My vote reveals mild trust in the successful team and acceptance of a reasonable low-risk proposal, which helps the good team more than it helps evil.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, so players 0,1,2 are good or Merlin/Percival. Quest 1 succeeded on team [2,0], so at least neither 0 nor 2 was forced to fail; both are more likely good. Current team [0,2,3] includes me but excludes my teammate 4, so it is a likely all-good-looking team with only one evil. As Minion, optimal play is usually to avoid looking suspicious early while preserving future sabotage opportunities. Rejecting a team with myself on it can look odd, and if approved I can choose fail on quest 2 to put evil on the board. My vote reveals alignment clues; approving is safer socially and keeps me positioned to influence the quest.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know player 4 is Evil. The proposed team [0,4] includes a known Evil, so approving risks an immediate fail on quest 3. Since Good is up 2-0, optimal play is to reject any team containing a known Evil while avoiding overly explicit signaling beyond normal good play. Rejecting is standard and protects Good more than it reveals.
Player 3: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0 and 2 have both been on successful teams, so they are currently read as good by the table. We are in team voting on quest 3 with a proposed 2-person team [0,4]. As evil, getting an evil player onto the quest is valuable, and this team includes my teammate 4 plus a trusted player 0, making it likely to pass and giving evil a chance to score. My approve vote supports that outcome; while approving may slightly associate me with 4 later, the benefit of sending 4 outweighs that.
Player 1: Players 0 and 2 were on the first successful team; 0,2,3 were on the second successful team. With two clean successes and no fails, players 0,2,3 all look reasonably good, while 4 is less tested. As a Good Servant in team voting on quest 3, optimal play is to prefer teams built from previously successful members when possible. This proposed team [0,4] drops both 2 and 3 for an untested 4, creating unnecessary risk. Rejecting preserves pressure for a safer team and reveals that I favor evidence-based compositions, which helps Good more than Evil.
Player 4: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 3. Two successful quests already put Evil in a bad spot. Proposed team is [0,4], which includes me but excludes my known evil teammate, so I can choose to fail quest 3 if approved. As an evil player, getting onto the quest is valuable; approving maximizes chances this team goes and gives me direct control. Voting approve is consistent with wanting an evil on the mission, and my vote alone reveals little because leaders often support their own teams.
Player 2: Players 0 and I (2) were on both successful quests, so 0 looks strongly good; 3 was also on a successful 3-person quest, while 4 is untested. As a good Servant, with two successes already, approving a team anchored by 0 is reasonable because this 2-person quest only needs one more success and there is no strong evidence against 4. Rejecting could fish for a 'safer' team like [0,2], but on round 1 an approve gives useful info on 4 without much downside since 0 is trusted. My vote signals moderate trust in 0 and willingness to test 4.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 3 and 4 are evil. Team [0,1,2] excludes both known evils, and prior results support 1 and 2 as likely good: [2,0] and [0,2,3] both succeeded, while [0,4] failed, strongly implicating 4. On team voting for quest 4, approving an all-good team is optimal and reveals little harmful information since as leader I already proposed the safest team.
Player 4: I know 3 is my evil teammate. Quest 3 team [0,4] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly implicates me or 0; since I know I failed it, 0 is actually good from my perspective. The proposed team [0,1,2] excludes both evils, so if approved it is very likely to succeed and put Good to 3 successes. As evil in team voting, optimal play is to reject clean all-good teams, especially on quest 4. My reject also aligns with trying to cast doubt after the prior fail without revealing extra role info.
Player 1: As a Servant, I know only public info. Team [0,2] succeeded twice, so 0 and 2 are relatively more trusted, though one could still be evil. Team [0,4] failed with exactly one fail, so at least one of 0 or 4 is evil, making 4 suspicious and slightly lowering confidence in 0. Proposed team [0,1,2] excludes 4, the strongest suspect, and includes me (good) plus 2, who has been on two successes. For quest 4 in 5-player Avalon, a 3-person team is required and good should favor teams built from the strongest public clears. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a team consistent with available evidence, and rejecting would risk discarding the best-looking team so far.
Player 3: I know player 4 is evil with me. Quest 3 team [0,4] had exactly 1 fail, so 0 could be good and 4 provided the fail; that makes [0,1,2] likely all-good, which is dangerous for evil because approving gives good a strong chance to reach 3 successes on quest 4. In team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject strong all-good teams, especially on quest 4 where a success would put good at match point or win depending on setup. My reject gives little extra info since evil are expected to oppose clean teams after a known fail involving 4.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. From quests: [2,0] succeeded so 0 and 2 look good; [0,2,3] succeeded so 3 also looks good relative to those teams. [0,4] failed with one fail, and since 0 was on two clean successes, 4 is the strongest evil suspect. Player 1 is still largely unknown. Current team [0,1,2] excludes the strongest suspect 4 and includes the two strongest good reads 0 and 2, with one unknown. On quest 4 with score 2-1, approving a team with two likely goods and no top suspect is best. My vote reveals trust in the strongest evidence-based team available and helps Good push toward a likely success.

Since 3 was on a success team, good may suspect 3 less, but as evil 3 is visible and thus not Merlin. Between 0,1,2, player 0 was on all successful quests except the failed one with me, making 0 the most central likely Merlin candidate. Optimal play as Assassin is to maximize chance of hitting Merlin; killing 0 is the best guess from the quest history. This reveals nothing further now beyond the assassination choice, so I should take the highest-probability shot.
